title: Policy Analysis: NYC Payroll Optimization Strategies
text:
This white paper presents an analysis of New York City’s payroll data with recommendations to optimize taxpayer spending. As a senior technical analyst for the Commission to Analyze Taxpayer Spending (CATS), I have identified potential cost-saving measures through three policy recommendations.



The job title with the highest base rate of pay is Chief Medical Examiner
NYPD has the highest average total annual payroll per employee
NYPD also has the most employees on payroll each year
Fire Department has the highest overtime usage compared to regular hours
The city’s aggregate payroll has grown significantly over the past 10 years

title: Policy Analysis: NYC Payroll Optimization Strategies
text:
The job title with the highest base rate of pay is Chief Medical Examiner
NYPD has the highest average total annual payroll per employee
NYPD also has the most employees on payroll each year
Fire Department has the highest overtime usage compared to regular hours
The city’s aggregate payroll has grown significantly over the past 10 years

title: Policy Analysis: NYC Payroll Optimization Strategies
Policy Analysis: NYC Payroll Optimization Strategies
text:
3.2 Data Preparation


Show code
# Task 2: Data Import and Preparation
library(readr)
payroll_data <- read_csv("data/mp01/nyc_payroll_export.csv")

# Transform string columns to title case
library(stringr)
payroll_data <- payroll_data %>%
  mutate(
    agency_name = str_to_title(agency_name),
    last_name = str_to_title(last_name),
    first_name = str_to_title(first_name),
    work_location_borough = str_to_title(work_location_borough),
    title_description = str_to_title(title_description),
    leave_status_as_of_june_30 = str_to_title(leave_status_as_of_june_30)
  )

# Check the data structure
glimpse(payroll_data)

title: Policy Analysis: NYC Payroll Optimization Strategies
text:
3.3 Total Compensation Calculation


Show code
# Task 3: Calculate Total Compensation
payroll_data <- payroll_data %>%
  mutate(
    total_compensation = case_when(
      pay_basis == "per Annum" ~ base_salary,
      pay_basis == "per Hour" ~ (regular_hours + ot_hours * 1.5) * hourly_rate,
      pay_basis == "per Day" ~ (regular_hours / 7.5) * daily_rate,
      TRUE ~ NA_real_
    )
  )

title: Policy Analysis: NYC Payroll Optimization Strategies
text:
5.1 Key Statistical Findings


Show code
# Which job title has the highest base rate of pay?
highest_base_rate <- payroll_data %>%
  filter(pay_basis == "per Annum") %>%
  mutate(hourly_equivalent = base_salary / 2000) %>%
  arrange(desc(hourly_equivalent)) %>%
  slice(1) %>%
  select(title_description, base_salary, hourly_equivalent)

highest_base_rate %>%
  mutate(
    base_salary = dollar(base_salary),
    hourly_equivalent = dollar(hourly_equivalent)
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Job Title with Highest Base Rate of Pay")




Show code
# Which individual & in what year had the single highest city total payroll?
highest_payroll <- payroll_data %>%
  group_by(first_name, last_name, fiscal_year) %>%
  summarize(
    total_payroll = sum(base_salary + total_ot_paid, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(total_payroll)) %>%
  slice(1)

highest_payroll %>%
  mutate(total_payroll = dollar(total_payroll)) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Individual with Highest Total Payroll")




Show code
# Which individual worked the most overtime hours?
individual_most_overtime <- payroll_data %>%
  arrange(desc(ot_hours)) %>%
  select(first_name, last_name, agency_name, fiscal_year, ot_hours) %>%
  slice(1)

individual_most_overtime %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Individual with Most Overtime Hours")




Show code
# Which agency has the highest average total annual payroll?
agency_payroll_avg <- payroll_data %>%
  group_by(agency_name) %>%
  summarise(
    average_total_payroll = mean(total_compensation, na.rm = TRUE),
    employee_count = n()
  ) %>%
  filter(employee_count >= 10) %>% # Filtering out very small departments
  arrange(desc(average_total_payroll)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 10)

agency_payroll_avg %>%
  mutate(average_total_payroll = dollar(average_total_payroll)) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Top 10 Agencies by Average Employee Payroll")




Show code
# Which agency has the most employees on payroll in each year?
agency_employee_count <- payroll_data %>%
  group_by(fiscal_year, agency_name) %>%
  summarise(employee_count = n(), .groups = 'drop') %>%
  group_by(fiscal_year) %>%
  slice_max(order_by = employee_count, n = 1) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  arrange(fiscal_year)

agency_employee_count %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Agency with Most Employees by Fiscal Year")




Show code
# Which agency has the highest overtime usage?
overtime_usage_ratio <- payroll_data %>%
  group_by(agency_name) %>%
  summarise(
    total_regular_hours = sum(regular_hours, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_overtime_hours = sum(ot_hours, na.rm = TRUE),
    overtime_ratio = total_overtime_hours / total_regular_hours,
    .groups = 'drop'
  ) %>%
  filter(total_regular_hours > 10000) %>% # Filter to ensure meaningful ratios
  arrange(desc(overtime_ratio)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 10)

overtime_usage_ratio %>%
  mutate(overtime_ratio = scales::percent(overtime_ratio, accuracy = 0.01)) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Top 10 Agencies by Overtime Usage Ratio")




Show code
# What is the average salary of employees who work outside the five boroughs?
average_salary_outside_boroughs <- payroll_data %>%
  filter(!work_location_borough %in% c("Manhattan", "Brooklyn", "Queens", "Bronx", "Staten Island")) %>%
  summarise(average_salary = mean(base_salary, na.rm = TRUE))

average_salary_outside_boroughs %>%
  mutate(average_salary = dollar(average_salary)) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Average Salary Outside NYC Boroughs")




Show code
# How much has the city's aggregate payroll grown over the past years?
payroll_growth <- payroll_data %>%
  group_by(fiscal_year) %>%
  summarise(total_payroll = sum(total_compensation, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  arrange(fiscal_year)

# Calculate the growth percentage
if(nrow(payroll_growth) >= 2) {
  earliest_year <- min(payroll_growth$fiscal_year)
  latest_year <- max(payroll_growth$fiscal_year)
  earliest_payroll <- payroll_growth$total_payroll[payroll_growth$fiscal_year == earliest_year]
  latest_payroll <- payroll_growth$total_payroll[payroll_growth$fiscal_year == latest_year]
  
  growth_percentage <- (latest_payroll - earliest_payroll) / earliest_payroll * 100
  
  # Create visualization
  ggplot(payroll_growth, aes(x = fiscal_year, y = total_payroll)) +
    geom_line(color = "blue", size = 1.2) +
    geom_point(color = "red", size = 3) +
    scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
    labs(
      title = "NYC Aggregate Payroll Growth",
      subtitle = paste0("Growth from ", earliest_year, " to ", latest_year, ": ", round(growth_percentage, 2), "%"),
      x = "Fiscal Year",
      y = "Total Payroll"
    ) +
    theme_minimal()
}

title: Policy Analysis: NYC Payroll Optimization Strategies
section: 6.1 Policy I: Capping Salaries at Mayoral Level
text:
6.1 Policy I: Capping Salaries at Mayoral Level


Show code
# Find mayoral salary for each fiscal year
mayoral_salaries <- payroll_data %>%
  filter(title_description == "Mayor") %>%
  group_by(fiscal_year) %>%
  summarize(mayoral_salary = max(base_salary, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# For simplicity, use the maximum mayoral salary across all years
mayoral_cap <- max(mayoral_salaries$mayoral_salary, na.rm = TRUE)

# Calculate potential savings from implementing the cap
salary_cap_analysis <- payroll_data %>%
  mutate(
    over_cap = total_compensation > mayoral_cap,
    savings = if_else(over_cap, total_compensation - mayoral_cap, 0)
  ) %>%
  summarize(
    employees_affected = sum(over_cap, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_savings = sum(savings, na.rm = TRUE),
    percent_of_workforce = employees_affected / n() * 100
  )

# Display the results
salary_cap_analysis %>%
  mutate(
    total_savings = dollar(total_savings),
    percent_of_workforce = scales::percent(percent_of_workforce/100, accuracy = 0.01)
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Potential Impact of Salary Cap at Mayoral Level")


Many governments implement policies that cap the salaries of public employees at the level of the chief executive. This analysis examines the potential impact of capping NYC employee compensation at the mayoral salary level.
Based on our analysis, implementing a salary cap at the mayoral level would affect a small percentage of employees but could generate significant savings. However, careful consideration must be given to potential impacts on recruitment and retention of specialized talent.

title: Policy Analysis: NYC Payroll Optimization Strategies
section: 6.2 Policy II: Increasing Staffing to Reduce Overtime Expenses
text:
6.2 Policy II: Increasing Staffing to Reduce Overtime Expenses


Show code
# Calculate overtime analysis by agency and job title
overtime_analysis <- payroll_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(hourly_rate), !is.na(ot_hours), ot_hours > 0) %>%
  mutate(
    overtime_cost = ot_hours * hourly_rate * 1.5,
    regular_cost_equivalent = ot_hours * hourly_rate,
    potential_savings = overtime_cost - regular_cost_equivalent,
    full_time_equivalents_needed = ot_hours / 2000  # Assuming 2000 hours per year
  ) %>%
  group_by(agency_name, title_description) %>%
  summarize(
    total_ot_hours = sum(ot_hours, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_overtime_cost = sum(overtime_cost, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_regular_cost_equivalent = sum(regular_cost_equivalent, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_potential_savings = sum(potential_savings, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_fte_needed = sum(full_time_equivalents_needed, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  filter(total_ot_hours > 1000) %>%  # Focus on significant overtime usage
  arrange(desc(total_potential_savings))

# Display top opportunities
overtime_analysis %>%
  slice_head(n = 10) %>%
  select(
    agency_name, 
    title_description, 
    total_ot_hours, 
    total_potential_savings,
    total_fte_needed
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    total_potential_savings = dollar(total_potential_savings),
    total_fte_needed = round(total_fte_needed, 1)
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Top 10 Opportunities for Overtime Reduction")


Overtime expenses significantly impact the city’s budget due to the 1.5x premium on overtime hours. This analysis examines whether hiring additional employees could reduce overtime costs.
Our analysis identifies significant potential savings from strategic hiring to replace overtime hours with regular-time work. Key departments like NYPD and Fire Department would benefit most from this approach.

title: Policy Analysis: NYC Payroll Optimization Strategies
section: 6.3 Policy III: Implementing Flexible Work Arrangements
text:
6.3 Policy III: Implementing Flexible Work Arrangements


Show code
# Analyze potential savings from capping overtime at 20 hours per employee
flexible_work_analysis <- payroll_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(hourly_rate), !is.na(ot_hours)) %>%
  mutate(
    current_ot_cost = ot_hours * hourly_rate * 1.5,
    capped_ot_hours = pmin(ot_hours, 20),  # Cap at 20 hours
    capped_ot_cost = capped_ot_hours * hourly_rate * 1.5,
    savings = current_ot_cost - capped_ot_cost
  ) %>%
  summarize(
    total_current_ot_cost = sum(current_ot_cost, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_capped_ot_cost = sum(capped_ot_cost, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_savings = sum(savings, na.rm = TRUE),
    employees_affected = sum(ot_hours > 20, na.rm = TRUE),
    percent_workforce_affected = employees_affected / n() * 100
  )

# Display results
flexible_work_analysis %>%
  mutate(
    total_current_ot_cost = dollar(total_current_ot_cost),
    total_capped_ot_cost = dollar(total_capped_ot_cost),
    total_savings = dollar(total_savings),
    percent_workforce_affected = scales::percent(percent_workforce_affected/100, accuracy = 0.01)
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Potential Impact of Flexible Work Policy with Overtime Cap")


As a third policy option, implementing flexible work arrangements with caps on overtime hours could provide both financial savings and improved work-life balance for city employees.
Our analysis shows that implementing a flexible work arrangement policy with overtime caps would generate significant savings while affecting only a portion of the workforce. This policy could also improve employee satisfaction and productivity.

title: Policy Analysis: NYC Payroll Optimization Strategies
text:
7.1 Limitations and Future Research
This analysis has several limitations:

Data quality issues, such as missing values and inconsistent coding
Inability to account for all factors affecting hiring decisions
Limited information on job market conditions for specialized roles

Future research could: - Compare NYC salaries with those in other major cities - Analyze the impact of collective bargaining agreements on overtime usage - Evaluate the effectiveness of flexible work arrangements in public sector settings

title: Policy Analysis: NYC Payroll Optimization Strategies
text:
9.1 Methodology Details
This analysis used R statistical software and the following packages for data processing and visualization:

dplyr, tidyr (data manipulation)
ggplot2 (visualization)
DT (interactive tables)
scales (formatting)

All code used in this analysis is available in the accompanying R Markdown document.

title: Policy Analysis: NYC Payroll Optimization Strategies
text:
9.2 Additional Tables and Figures


Show code
# Top job titles by average compensation
top_titles <- payroll_data %>%
  group_by(title_description) %>%
  summarize(
    count = n(),
    avg_compensation = mean(total_compensation, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  filter(count >= 50) %>%  # Only include common job titles
  arrange(desc(avg_compensation)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 15)

ggplot(top_titles, aes(x = reorder(title_description, avg_compensation), y = avg_compensation)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
  labs(
    title = "Top 15 Job Titles by Average Compensation",
    subtitle = "Only includes titles with at least 50 employees",
    x = "Job Title",
    y = "Average Total Compensation"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

title: Welcome
text:
About Me
Hello! I’m La Maria, a global citizen who’s been lucky enough to call some amazing cities home - from the bustling streets of São Paulo to the artistic vibes of Barcelona, the culinary paradise of Modena, the tech-driven San Francisco, the charming Brussels, and now the endless energy of New York City! 🌎
I’m a Business Development professional with fifteen years of experience in event management and sales, currently pursuing my Master’s in Business Analytics at Baruch College. When I’m not crunching numbers, you’ll find me exploring the intersection of data and human behavior (yes, I’m that kind of nerd, but a fun one! 😄).

“Very little is needed to make a happy life; it is all within yourself, in your way of thinking.” - Marcus Aurelius

This quote from Marcus Aurelius pretty much sums up my life philosophy. Speaking of which…

title: Welcome
text:
My Daily Philosophy
When I’m not diving into datasets or organizing community events, you’ll find me: - Running through Central Park at dawn (sometimes having philosophical debates with myself about whether that extra mile is really necessary 😅) - Gardening my small but mighty urban jungle (where I practice patience and learn that not everything can be controlled by spreadsheets) - Traveling to discover new perspectives (and the best local coffee shops) - Reading philosophy, especially Stoicism (Marcus Aurelius is my go-to life coach from 2000 years ago)
I speak English, Italian, Spanish, and Portuguese - which means I can discuss data analytics in four languages, but I prefer to talk about food in Italian! 🍝

title: Welcome
text:
Career Highlights

Data Analytics Coordinator at the United Nations Staff Recreation Latin Club Society: Led a team in developing a data-driven marketing strategy that increased membership and boosted website traffic.
Internship at LaGuardia CUNY Department of Humanities & Health: Applied data management techniques to organize and analyze research data, contributing to inclusive healthcare practices.

title: Welcome
text:
Education

Master’s Degree in Business Analytics - Expected Fall 2025, Baruch Zicklin, New York, NY
Bachelor’s Degree in Communication and Media Studies - Spring 2024, CUNY School of Professional Studies, New York, NY

title: Welcome
text:
My Favorite Playlist
Music is the soundtrack of my life journey, here’s what keeps me moving:
1. Piano Sonata No. 14 (Moonlight Sonata) - Ludwig van Beethoven (perfect for late-night coding sessions)
2. Non, je ne regrette rien - Edith Piaf (my power song for challenging days)

4. L’Amore si muove - Il Volo (reminds me of my time in Italy)
